name: Burkina Faso
tagline: From the Mossi kingdoms to the land of upright people
summary: For centuries the central plateau of the upper Volta basin was held by the Mossi kingdoms — Ouagadougou, Yatenga and Tenkodogo — ruled by the Mogho Naba and famed for their cavalry and their long resistance to Islamisation and to the Mali and Songhai empires, while Gurma, Bobo, Lobi and other peoples held the surrounding lands. Conquered by France in the 1890s, the territory was governed as the colony of Upper Volta, created in 1919, partitioned in 1932 and reconstituted in 1947. It gained independence as Upper Volta on 5 August 1960, endured a cycle of coups, was renamed Burkina Faso during Thomas Sankara's revolution in 1984, and after the long rule and 2014 overthrow of Blaise Compaoré faced a grinding Sahelian jihadist insurgency and renewed military rule.

founding.label: Independence from France
founding.detail: The Republic of Upper Volta gained full independence from France on 5 August 1960 under its first president, Maurice Yaméogo; the country was renamed Burkina Faso during Thomas Sankara's revolution in 1984.

quickFacts.label: Renamed
quickFacts.value: Burkina Faso, 4 August 1984 ('land of upright people')

quickFacts.label: Official / working language
quickFacts.value: French; about 60 national languages (Mooré, Dioula, Fulfulde and others)

eras.title: The Mossi Kingdoms
eras.standfirst: On the upper Volta plateau, cavalry states under the Mogho Naba hold their ground against the great Sahelian empires.
body: From around the fifteenth century — though Mossi tradition and some scholars place the dynastic origins earlier — a cluster of kingdoms took shape on the central plateau of the upper Volta basin. Tradition traces them to the princess Yennenga and her son Ouédraogo, whose descendants founded the principal Mossi states of Tenkodogo, Ouagadougou and, later, Yatenga. The ruler of Ouagadougou bore the title Mogho Naba — rendered as 'king of the world' — and stood as the senior sovereign among the Mossi, surrounded by an elaborate court and a hierarchy of district chiefs (naba) who held divinely sanctioned authority (naam).

The Mossi were among the few peoples of the western Sudan to resist both conquest and conversion by the surrounding Muslim empires. Mounted Mossi warriors raided north into the Niger bend, and when the Songhai emperor Askia Muhammad I launched campaigns against them around the close of the fifteenth century, demanding their submission to Islam, the Mossi refused and preserved their own religious and political order. Their cavalry made them a formidable regional power even as Mali and Songhai dominated the trade cities to the north.

Beyond the Mossi heartland lived many other peoples — the Gurma to the east, the Bobo and Bwa to the west, the Lobi and Gurunsi to the south, and Fulani (Peul) herders across the north. Some, like the Lohron and Kulango associated with the stone enclosures of the south-west, prospered from the trans-Saharan gold trade. With the decline of central authority in the eighteenth century, Yatenga, centred on Ouahigouya, became fully independent of Ouagadougou, and the Mossi states entered the nineteenth century as a patchwork of largely autonomous kingdoms.

events.title: Foundation of the Mossi states
events.summary: Tradition attributes the Mossi kingdoms of Tenkodogo, Ouagadougou and Yatenga to the line of Ouédraogo, son of the princess Yennenga; the dating is uncertain and some accounts place the origins earlier.

events.title: Songhai campaigns against the Mossi
events.summary: The Songhai emperor Askia Muhammad I campaigns against the non-Muslim Mossi, defeating Mossi of Yatenga but failing to subdue the kingdoms, which retain their religion and independence.

events.title: Yatenga becomes independent of Ouagadougou
events.summary: As central authority weakens, the kingdom of Yatenga, with its capital at Ouahigouya, breaks free of Ouagadougou's overlordship.

pullquote.text: They were one of the few kingdoms to successfully resist the spread of Islam while controlling trade between the empires around them.
pullquote.attribution: on the Mossi, EBSCO Research Starters

eras.title: Loropéni and the Gold Frontier
eras.standfirst: In the south-west, fortified stone enclosures testify to a society grown wealthy on the trans-Saharan gold trade.
body: While the Mossi held the plateau, the south-west of present-day Burkina Faso was the setting for a different chapter. Near the modern town of Loropéni stand the best-preserved of roughly a hundred ruined stone enclosures scattered across the Lobi country. Built of laterite walls reaching up to about six metres high, the Loropéni complex protected a settlement whose wealth was tied to the long-distance commerce in West African gold.

The enclosures are associated with the Lohron (Loron) and Kulango peoples, who appear to have controlled the extraction and trade of gold feeding the trans-Saharan caravan routes. The Loropéni site is thought to be at least a thousand years old, with the surrounding network of fortresses reaching its height between roughly the fourteenth and seventeenth centuries, when the gold trade was most intense.

The settlement was abandoned in the early nineteenth century, its builders' identity and the reasons for its decline still imperfectly understood. In 2009 the Ruins of Loropéni became Burkina Faso's first UNESCO World Heritage Site, recognised as a rare and substantial monument to the medieval gold economy that linked the forest edge of West Africa to the markets of North Africa.

events.title: Loropéni's gold-trade fortresses flourish
events.summary: The stone enclosures of the Lobi country, including Loropéni, reach their height as the trans-Saharan gold trade peaks; the settlements are linked to the Lohron and Kulango peoples.

events.title: Loropéni settlement abandoned
events.summary: The fortified settlement at Loropéni is abandoned, for reasons still debated by archaeologists.

events.title: Loropéni inscribed by UNESCO
events.summary: The Ruins of Loropéni are added to the UNESCO World Heritage List, becoming Burkina Faso's first such site.

eras.title: Conquest and the Colony of Upper Volta
eras.standfirst: France seizes the Volta lands in the 1890s; a colony is created, dismembered, then reborn on the road to self-rule.
body: French expansion reached the Mossi country in the 1890s. France claimed the region from 1896, and resistance ended with the occupation of the Mossi capital, Ouagadougou; the deposed Mogho Naba Wobgo (Boukary Koutou) fled and died in exile in 1904. The conquered lands were incorporated into French West Africa, where colonial rule brought forced labour, head taxes and the conscription of Voltaic men for the cotton economy and for France's armies.

On 1 March 1919 France carved out a distinct colony of Upper Volta (Haute-Volta) from neighbouring territories, recognising the dense Mossi population as a useful administrative and labour reserve. In 1932, however, the colony was dissolved 'to economise', its territory partitioned among Côte d'Ivoire, French Sudan and Niger — a move that channelled Voltaic labour toward the plantations of the Ivory Coast. Mossi leaders, including the Mogho Naba, pressed for the colony's restoration.

After the Second World War those demands were met. On 4 September 1947 Upper Volta was reconstituted within its earlier boundaries as a territory of French West Africa. Post-war reforms brought elected representation and rising nationalist politics; in December 1958 Upper Volta became a self-governing republic within the French Community, the final step before full independence.

events.title: French conquest of the Mossi
events.summary: France claims the Volta region and occupies Ouagadougou; Mossi resistance collapses and the Mogho Naba Wobgo goes into exile.

events.title: Colony of Upper Volta created
events.summary: France establishes the separate colony of Upper Volta (Haute-Volta) within French West Africa.

events.title: Upper Volta partitioned
events.summary: The colony is dismembered for economic reasons and divided among Côte d'Ivoire, French Sudan and Niger.

events.title: Upper Volta reconstituted
events.summary: After the war and Mossi pressure, Upper Volta is re-established within its former boundaries as a French West African territory.

pullquote.text: Dismembered in 1932 to economise, the colony's lands — and its people's labour — were parcelled out to its neighbours.
pullquote.attribution: on the partition of Upper Volta

eras.title: Independence and the Cycle of Coups
eras.standfirst: Upper Volta wins independence in 1960, then settles into two decades of fragile civilian rule punctuated by military seizures of power.
body: On 5 August 1960 the Republic of Upper Volta became fully independent of France, with Maurice Yaméogo of the Voltaic Democratic Union (UDV) as its first president. Yaméogo soon banned rival parties and concentrated power, but austerity measures provoked a popular revolt; in January 1966 he resigned and the army chief of staff, Lieutenant Colonel Sangoulé Lamizana, took over, opening a long era in which the military repeatedly intervened.

Lamizana dominated the 1970s, alternating between military and semi-civilian government and weathering severe Sahelian droughts. His rule ended in a coup on 25 November 1980 led by Colonel Saye Zerbo. Zerbo was himself overthrown in November 1982, bringing Major Jean-Baptiste Ouédraogo to the head of state.

Within Ouédraogo's government a faction of radical young officers gained influence, foremost among them the charismatic captain Thomas Sankara, who served briefly as prime minister in early 1983 before being arrested. His detention triggered a counter-move by his allies, setting the stage for a revolution that would transform the country.

events.title: Independence of Upper Volta
events.summary: Upper Volta gains full independence from France; Maurice Yaméogo becomes its first president.

events.title: Fall of Yaméogo; Lamizana takes power
events.summary: A popular revolt against austerity forces President Yaméogo to resign; Lieutenant Colonel Sangoulé Lamizana leads the army into government.

events.title: Coup brings Saye Zerbo to power
events.summary: Colonel Saye Zerbo overthrows Lamizana, continuing the pattern of military seizures of power.

events.title: Jean-Baptiste Ouédraogo takes over
events.summary: A further coup ousts Zerbo and installs Major Jean-Baptiste Ouédraogo, in whose government radical young officers, including Thomas Sankara, gain influence.

eras.title: Sankara's Revolution and the Birth of Burkina Faso
eras.standfirst: A young captain seizes power, renames the nation 'land of upright people', and drives a radical programme of self-reliance — until his assassination.
body: On 4 August 1983 Thomas Sankara, freed by allied paratroopers, took power in a coup at the age of thirty-three. A Marxist and Pan-Africanist who admired Castro and Guevara, he launched a sweeping revolution aimed at self-reliance and the uplift of the rural poor. On 4 August 1984, the first anniversary of his accession, he renamed the country Burkina Faso — drawing on Mooré and Dioula to mean 'land of upright (honest) people' — and gave it a new flag and anthem.

Sankara's government mounted mass vaccination campaigns, built schools, clinics and railways with volunteer labour, and planted millions of trees against the advancing desert. He redistributed land, promoted women's rights — appointing women to government, opposing female genital cutting and forced marriage — and set up popular tribunals to pursue corruption. Rejecting foreign aid and debt as instruments of dependence, he famously urged African states to refuse to repay their debts. A brief border 'Christmas War' with Mali over the Agacher Strip flared in December 1985.

The revolution's austerity, its purges and its confrontations with unions and traditional authorities also bred opposition, and Sankara's relations with his closest comrade, Blaise Compaoré, deteriorated. On 15 October 1987 Sankara was killed, along with twelve others, in a coup mounted by Compaoré, who seized the presidency. Decades later, in 2022, a Burkinabè court convicted Compaoré in absentia of complicity in the assassination.

events.title: Sankara seizes power
events.summary: Captain Thomas Sankara comes to power in a coup, beginning a radical revolution oriented toward self-reliance and the rural poor.

events.title: Upper Volta renamed Burkina Faso
events.summary: On the first anniversary of the revolution, the country is renamed Burkina Faso — 'land of upright (honest) people' — with a new flag and anthem.

events.title: Agacher Strip war with Mali
events.summary: A brief border conflict, the 'Christmas War', is fought with Mali over the mineral-rich Agacher Strip before regional mediation ends it.

events.title: Assassination of Thomas Sankara
events.summary: Sankara and twelve others are killed in a coup led by his comrade Blaise Compaoré, who takes the presidency.

figures.name: Thomas Sankara
figures.blurb: Marxist and Pan-Africanist captain who seized power in 1983, renamed the country Burkina Faso, and pursued radical reforms in health, the environment, women's rights and self-reliance before his assassination in 1987.

pullquote.text: He renamed the country Burkina Faso — 'land of upright people' — and planted ten million trees against the desert.

eras.title: Compaoré, Uprising and the Sahel Insurgency
eras.standfirst: A 27-year presidency ends in a popular revolt, after which jihadist violence engulfs the north and the military returns to power.
body: Blaise Compaoré reversed much of Sankara's programme, opening the economy and aligning with Western and regional partners while ruling for twenty-seven years. He won repeated, lopsided elections, but his rule was shadowed by repression — symbolised by the 1998 murder of the investigative journalist Norbert Zongo — and by the unsolved killing of Sankara. In late 2014 Compaoré moved to amend the constitution so he could run again.

The attempt provoked a popular uprising. On 30 October 2014 protesters stormed and burned the National Assembly in Ouagadougou; amid mass demonstrations Compaoré resigned the next day, 31 October, and fled to Côte d'Ivoire. After a transitional period — including a brief coup by the presidential guard in September 2015 — Roch Marc Christian Kaboré was elected president in November 2015.

From 2015 Burkina Faso was drawn into the wider Sahelian jihadist insurgency, with attacks by groups linked to al-Qaeda (notably JNIM), the Islamic State (ISGS) and the homegrown Ansarul Islam spreading from the north and east. Thousands were killed and well over a million people displaced. Citing the state's failure to contain the violence, the army seized power on 24 January 2022 under Lieutenant Colonel Paul-Henri Damiba; eight months later, on 30 September 2022, Captain Ibrahim Traoré overthrew Damiba in a second coup, taking power amid a still-spreading insurgency.

events.title: Murder of Norbert Zongo
events.summary: The investigative journalist Norbert Zongo is killed, a case that becomes a symbol of repression under Compaoré and triggers mass protests.

events.title: Popular uprising ousts Compaoré
events.summary: Protests against Compaoré's bid to extend his rule culminate in the storming of the National Assembly; he resigns after 27 years and flees to Côte d'Ivoire.

events.title: Jihadist insurgency spreads to Burkina Faso
events.summary: Sahelian jihadist groups linked to al-Qaeda and the Islamic State, alongside the homegrown Ansarul Islam, escalate attacks, killing thousands and displacing more than a million people.

events.title: Coup ousts President Kaboré
events.summary: Citing the government's failure to halt the insurgency, the military under Lieutenant Colonel Paul-Henri Damiba removes elected president Roch Marc Christian Kaboré.

events.title: Second 2022 coup brings Ibrahim Traoré to power
events.summary: Captain Ibrahim Traoré overthrows Damiba in a second coup, becoming transitional leader as jihadist violence continues to spread.

figures.name: Blaise Compaoré
figures.blurb: Came to power in the 1987 coup that killed Thomas Sankara and ruled for 27 years until a popular uprising forced his resignation in 2014; later convicted in absentia over Sankara's assassination.

pullquote.text: After twenty-seven years in power, he fell in a single tumultuous week of street protest.
pullquote.attribution: on the 2014 ousting of Blaise Compaoré

figures.name: Mogho Naba
figures.life: title in use since the 15th century
figures.blurb: Hereditary 'king of the world' who reigned over the senior Mossi kingdom of Ouagadougou; the office survives today as a moral and traditional authority in Burkina Faso.

figures.name: Thomas Sankara
figures.blurb: Pan-Africanist captain who seized power in 1983, renamed Upper Volta as Burkina Faso, and pursued radical self-reliance reforms before his assassination in 1987.

figures.name: Ibrahim Traoré
figures.blurb: Army captain who took power in the second coup of September 2022, becoming transitional president as Burkina Faso struggled against a widening Sahelian jihadist insurgency.
